text.content:
Book of the Wider World — p.228-235

The Steplands (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.DBEagy54rlf52AOz]{372}) are home to dozens of nomadic bands, collectively called the Hillfolk. The bands are tied together into a bewildering tapestry of kinship and intermarriage and treaty, rivalry and blood feud and long-standing grudge.

Some Hillfolk roam the Flats (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.7Rj5pjaWepSJPAms]{126}) in warmer months. Some bands trade with Stonetop, at least on occasion, signaling to them at Titan Bones (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.KZhJUg313MniTtl5]{448}). Others trade with Marshedge (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.eHmYRGms3Q9bzY70]{274}), and still others with southern merchants (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.QcBQpzqDPNBoCyww]{259}). They have little contact with the Manmarchers (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.V37UjFD9BlDh60JY]{282}), and tend to treat them with suspicion.

Their wariness towards the Manmarchers is nothing, though, compared to their contempt for Gordin’s Delve (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.93L1wwoxn0M4ANCL]{190}). The Hillfolk view mining metal ores as sacrilege. They’ll gladly steal from the Delvers or loot their corpses, but only the most debased Hillfolk trade peacefully with Gordin’s Delve. And only the most shunned and shamed will actually move there and call the mining town home.

Names

Breton-inspired, but missing vowels and clipped-sounding: Adm, Blej, Cirl, Davth, Elst, Gwilm, Gwenl, Henri, Ines, Jenfir, Jown, Juda, Kiln, Laurl, Loic, Merrn, Maikl, Nanzl, Nolwn, Quent, Reegn, Ropr, Sabi, Stren, Yanz.

Questions

  • Which of you knows the Hillfolk best? How so?
  • What clothing/markings/traits are distinctive to (these) Hillfolk?
  • How is Hillfolk language different
  • from Stonetop’s?
  • What do the Hillfolk call themselves?
  • What do they call each other?
  • What are groups of Hillfolk called? Bands? Rides? Herds? Families? Etc.
  • What affiliations do they have beyond their bands? Clans, nations, sects, etc?
  • What sparked the last open conflict between them and Gordin’s Delve?
  • What, do you think, is the source of
  • their taboo against mining?
  • What deity (if any) do they hold in
  • highest regard?
  • How is their view of the gods different
  • than Stonetop’s?
  • What festival do (these) Hillfolk
  • celebrate this time of year?
  • What game/pastime/food are (these) Hillfolk known for?

Hooks

  • A band of Hillfolk has come under the sway of a hdour (page 234) and moved into the Ruined Tower (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.Dp02ChmHXxEwVD75]{332}).
  • Ne’er-do-wells from Gordin’s Delve kill some Hillfolk in the Flats; war looms.
  • Someone in town owes a blood debt to a Hillfolk band; they come calling to collect, with friends.
  • The ruin that the PCs seek lies in the territory of a particularly unfriendly Hillfolk band.

Lore

Hillfolk rarely discuss their ancestry with outsiders. They claim an ancient right to the Steplands, but fear the barrow mounds (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.Qir4rs4BUipTSyFQ]{39}) and many of the Stone Lord (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.UyUHhvUYqOaGERlu]{382}) ruins.

Something interesting: The Hillfolk’s ancestors were human servants (slaves?) of the Stone Lords. They rebelled, calling on the power of the Things Below (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HurhcWKZBn60Vcq3]{412}). Petty warlords and would-be sorcerer-kings—the Barrow Builders (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.Qir4rs4BUipTSyFQ]{34})—dominated the Hillfolk for many years after the Stone Lords fell.

Something useful: Many Hillfolk traditions stem from their time suffering under the Stone Lords and the Barrow Builders, and the efforts required to throw off the yoke of those rulers. What’s one such tradition that you know of, and how can you use it to your advantage?

Nomads

Most Hillfolk bands are nomadic, following food and good grazing for their herds as the seasons change. Each band has traditional territory, including a winter camp with permanent shelter (caves, ruins, stone huts, wooden longhouses, etc.). A number of bands have territory that extends into the Flats or South Manmarch (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.hx1EiGo418JBGmVD]{350}), but winter camps are always nestled in the Steplands.

The Makers’ Roads (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.FEIURyGVNdxdU9vY]{270}) are considered communal territory, but otherwise infringing on a band’s territory is cause for suspicion or bloodshed. Wise travelers hide their passage, or else bring tribute and actively seek to avoid offence.

  • How do (these) Hillfolk mark the boundaries of their territory?
  • What sort of temporary structures do (these) Hillfolk dwell in? (Tents? Yurts? Log houses? Something else?)
  • The Steplands are fertile enough—why don’t (more) Hillfolk take up agriculture, do you think?

Hillfolk bands

Here’s a typical band:

Size hamlet (~25 to 75 souls)
Population +0
  • Prosperity -1
  • Trade with other bands
  • Trade with (pick 1)...
    ... Marshedge (rope, textiles, herbs, pottery)
    ... Stonetop (whisky, potatoes, oats)
    ... Lygos, etc. (salt, tools)
Resources
  • Horses, sheep, goats, hounds
  • Wool, milk
  • Forage (fruits, nuts, veggies, honey)
  • Hunting (meat, fur, hides)
  • Gathering (timber, sap, flint)
Defenses +0
  • Mounted warriors
  • Flint-tipped weapons (crude)

About a third of each band are able-bodied adults; the rest are children or elders. Maybe half of the adults are riders, and the rest manage life back at camp. Older children typically manage herds, often with the help of hounds. Specialization is rare, and even homemakers and older children are capable fighters.

  • Smoke from multiple fires seen over the horizon
  • Bleating of sheep or goats, cluck of chickens, bark of dogs
  • Thickets and pens for keeping herds
  • Stern, suspicious faces and hands hovering near weapons

If a PC is familiar with the(se) Hillfolk, ask them:

  • What is this group called?
  • What sets them apart from other Hillfolk?
  • Who are their staunchest allies?
  • Who are their bitterest rivals/enemies?
  • What resource do they have in (relative) abundance?

Naming bands

If you need inspiration for naming a Hillfolk band (or other group), pick a word from below (e.g. “Red band”) or mix 2-3 (e.g. “Ash Pickers” or “Ochre Wolf Fang”).

Arrow, Ash, Badger, Black, Blanket, Blue, Cloak, Crow, Drake, Eastern, Eye, Face, Fang, Feather, Ferret, Fist, Foot, Fur, Goat, Grass, Green, Hair, Hand, Herd(er), Hill, Hoof, Hound, Leaf, Lower, Moon, Northern, Ochre, Paw, Picker, Planter, Rain, Ram, Raven, Red, Rock, Run(ner), Seed, Shield, Shrew, Slayer, Snake, Snow, Soil, Southern, Spear, Staff, Sun, Tooth, Tree, Upper, Vulture, Watch(er), Weed, Western, Whisper(er), White, Wing, Woad, Wolf, Yellow.

Traits

Ask the PCs, or pick 1 or 2, or roll the Die of Fate.

@DrawTable[Compendium.stonetop.wonder-tables.RollTable.dXLPfgcoCrUmFDGz]{1d12}Trait

1Particularly large or small, prosperous or poor
2“Picked band,” made of unrelated adults, few children among them
3Worship a particular deity, revere a particular spirit
4Famous member, now or in the past
5Keep dangerous beasts (boars, drakes, etc.)
6Ride something other than horses (aurochs, Huffel rams, big deer)
7Tend to be large/slender/tall/ short/etc.
8Especially fierce/friendly/territorial/aggressive/political/etc.
9Extraordinarily large/fast/clever/ fearless/etc. horses
10Produce more than their share of spirit-talkers/hdours/gouzadn (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.DBEagy54rlf52AOz]{378})
11Guard a greatly feared and cursed ruin/barrow/area
12Not actually nomadic; they stay in cliff-dwellings all year

Trade & Barter

Commonly available

The following are special items in Stonetop, but commonly available among the Hillfolk—PCs can acquire them here without having to roll. They do need to trade something of equal Value. Stonetop trade goods (whisky, potatoes, oats) are always in demand.

Note that items printed on the Inventory insert are crude if acquired from the Hillfolk, and any iron items will instead be stone, bone, wooden, or maybe hammered copper. Iron items are considered special items (see below).

GoodsValue
Salt (a little box).0
Adz (hand, crude)0
Cudgel, wood (close, crude)0
Cartload of timber (immobile)2
LivestockValue
Goat (sure-footed, curious, hungry); HP 3; Damage d4 (hand); Instinct to explore; can butcher for provisions (6 uses) ◇1
Sheep (timid, hardy, wooly) HP 3; Damage d4 (hand); Instinct to follow the herd; can butcher for provisions (6 uses) ◇1
Sheep hound, follower (keen-nosed, herder, fierce) HP 6; Damage d6 (hand, grabby); Instinct to bully, threaten; Cost training1
Horse, follower (powerful, keen-nosed, swift or hardy, large); HP 10; Damage d6+3 (hand, close, forceful); Instinct to panic; Cost care & grooming3

Trade opportunities

If the PCs get a trade opportunity (from the Seasons Change move), a band of Hillfolk might offer the following:

  • A good exchange rate, two or even three Value 2 items for 1 Surplus.
  • A cartload of timber or a dozen sheep/goats (Value 2), in exchange for help with a problem.
  • Horses (Value 3) traded for a similar amount of Surplus (Value 2), plus help with a problem.
  • Someone versed in training and caring for horses, willing to move to Stonetop
  • An arcanum of the Stone Lords (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.UyUHhvUYqOaGERlu]{382}), the ﻿Barrow Builders (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.Qir4rs4BUipTSyFQ]{34}), the ﻿spirits of the wild (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HOWpYxmR8Q44qS3E]{356}), or the ﻿Things Below (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HurhcWKZBn60Vcq3]{412}).

Special items

The following might be acquired among the Hillfolk, but the PCs must roll Trade & Barter to acquire them. This is in addition to the special items on the Moves & Gear handout.

When the PCs Trade & Barter among the Hillfolk, they use the local Prosperity (-1 by default) with disadvantage for being a hamlet. On a 6-, they don’t mark XP and this band doesn’t have/want it—maybe they know of another band who might?

GoodsValue
Iron items from inventory insert0
Makerglass (misshapen shard/chunk) Makerglass (an item made of it) +21
LivestockValue
Huffel ram (hardy, wooly, powerful, grumpy); HP 6; Damage d6+2 (hand, forceful); Instinct to protect the herd; can butcher for provisions (9 uses) ◇2
Upland horse, follower? (powerful, keen-nosed, agile, brave, large); HP 14; Damage d6+3 (hand, close, forceful); Instinct to suffer no fools; Cost firm training3

Services and favors

PCs don’t need to roll Trade & Barter for the following, but they might need to Persuade the Hillfolk—even if they offer something of suitable Value. A group that’s well-disposed to the PCs might grant these boons for free, or without needing to be Persuaded, or at a reduced price.

Service/favorValue
Safe passage through their territory1
A few nights’ hospitality in their camp1
A guide through their territory2
A guide through another band’s territory3
A guide to a cursed/sacred/forbidden place3

Dangers

Hdour
Solitary, cautious, devious, hoarder,
magical, spirit-wise
HP 14; Armor 2 (thick hides, minor wards)
Damage bronze knife d8 (hand)
Instinct to ignore rules, boundaries, and taboos
  • Call up a spirit to attend them
  • Send spirits or beasts to harass, spy, or attack
  • Reveal some spell or trick prepared in advance
  • Unleash power they cannot control
Spirit-talkers (next page) have great
power, but use it with care and only for
the benefit of their people. There are many
rules that they follow, many things they
will refuse to do. Because to step over the
line, and to seek power for power’s sake,
and to use their power selfishly—that is
the way of the sorcerer, the hdour.
When the Hillfolk suspect a spirit-talker
of sorcery, they exile them. In the worst
cases, they bury them alive. Few are willing
to deal a death-blow to a hdour, for that’s a
sure way to get oneself cursed.
Hdour are often in possession of one or
more arcana (page 508), which they do
not hesitate to use. They are often at-
tended by beasts, spirits of the wild
(page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HOWpYxmR8Q44qS3E]{356}), spirits of the dead
and undying (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.sNLYfvJEFM1v463y]{68}),
and minions of the Things
Below (page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.HurhcWKZBn60Vcq3]{412}).
Hillfolk rider
Group, stealthy, organized, horse-wise
HP 6; Armor 0 to 2 (thick hides, shield)
Damage flint-tipped spear d8 (close, thrown, crude), cudgel d8 (close, crude), adz d8 (hand, crude), or bow d8 (near)
Instinct to distrust outsiders
  • Ride a steed like an extension of themselves
  • Outmaneuver their prey
  • Take advantage of the terrain
A typical band of Hillfolk has between 6
to 12 riders: adult hunters and warriors.
They patrol their territories in twos and
threes, seeking game and keeping an eye
out for trespassers. The brash and the bold
will challenge anyone they don’t recognize.
More cautious riders will follow unseen
and take the outsiders’ measure; whether
they let them pass, or spook them into
leaving, or murder them in their sleep…
well that remains to be seen.
When a Hillfolk rider and their horse attack
a foe together, they deal 1d6+4 damage (the
horse’s 1d6+3, with +1 for the rider), com-
bining tags from the attacks as makes sense.
Spirit-talker
Group, cautious, cunning, magical,
spirit-wise
HP 8; Armor 0 to 2 (thick hides, shield)
Damage flint-tipped spear d8 (close, thrown, crude), club or adz d8 (hand, crude), or bow d8 (near)
Special quality sensitive to the presence of active spirits
Instinct to protect their people
  • Sharply rebuke a spirit or mortal
  • Commune with beasts, or the spirits of a place or thing
  • Take advantage of the terrain
  • Borrow and wield a friendly spirit’s power
Now and then, a young Hillfolk shows signs
of being touched by the spirits. The signs
often come after a sickness or brush with
death. They hear voices that aren’t there.
They find themselves more comfortable
with horse and hound than their
fellow people. Sometimes, they lose
the human tongue altogether and
go feral, becoming a gouzadn
(page @UUID[Compendium.stonetop.wider-world-and-other-wonders.JournalEntry.DBEagy54rlf52AOz]{378}). But when things
go right—when they are
guided by an elder, and pos-
sessed of great self-control—
they become a spirit-talker
for their band.
Upland horse
Group, large, powerful, keen-nosed,
agile, brave
HP 14; Armor 0
Damage kick, trample d6+3 (hand, close, forceful)
Instinct to suffer no fools
  • Sniff trouble on the wind
  • Navigate tricky terrain or obstacles
  • Remain calm, unmoved
  • Find their own way
Hillfolk bands breed their horses for
strength, agility, and courage. The best
become steeds for Hillfolk riders. It takes
a firm hand and a steady will to handle an
Upland, but win one over and you’ve got a
friend for life.
